what: (Optional) Generate code for deSolve (ODE solver in R):
title: Build and simulate your own Model
text: also possible create R code, intended use deSolve. Combining previous function calls one (writing file immediately). test-simulation deSolve:
code: rFile <- paste0(comment(odeModel),".R") cat(uqsa::generateRCode(odeModel),sep="\n",file=rFile) source(rFile)  # example call: p <- AKAR4cl_default(0.0) y0 <- AKAR4cl_init(0.0,p)  print(y0) #> AKAR4p      C  #>      0      0 if (require(deSolve)){     t_ <- seq(0,50,length.out=128)     y_ <- ode(y0,t_,AKAR4cl_vf,p)     plot(t_,y_[,1],type='l',main="AKAR4cl",xlab="time") } #> Loading required package: deSolve

title: Build and simulate your own Model
text: now show simulate model conditions experiments (e.g., initial conditions). details simulating model can found page “Simulating model”.  simulation result sr list several components, including: state, state variable trajectories func, output functions output functions usually correspond data (way), may need sort normalization. example, relationship direct. state func three indices: corresponds experiment l, state variable , time-point j, parameter column k (relevant par matrix multiple columns). previously created modelName variable comment attribute (can also set , magic ). attribute indicates location shared library used simulations. , ytou don’t re-make every time - long model doesn’t change (.e., checkModel need re-called):
code: # import the experimental data ex <- SBtabVFGEN::sbtab.data(sb,odeModel$conservationLaws)  ## we create a closure called "sim" a function that internally remembers the "experiments" (ex) sim <- simulator.c(ex,modelName)                   # ex holds the instructions for the solver par <- as.matrix(sb$Parameter[["!DefaultValue"]])  # one column - but can be several sets (as columns)  ## sim can be called with any parameter vector - or several and will always simulate the same set of experiments sr <- sim(par)                                     # simulation results (one per experiment) stopifnot(length(sr) == length(ex))  i <- seq(70) t <- ex[[1]]$outputTimes[i] y <- ex[[1]]$outputValues[[1]][i] dy <- ex[[1]]$errorValues[[1]][i]  z <- sr[[1]]$func[1,i,1]                           # third dimension is the par column (just one here)  plot(t,z,bty='n',type='l', ylim=c(90,200)) points(t,y) arrows(t,y,t,y+dy,angle=90, length=0.01) arrows(t,y,t,y-dy,angle=90, length=0.01) legend("bottomright",legend=c("simulation","data"), lty=c(1,1), pch=c(NA,1)) sr[[l]]$state[i,j,k]        # is a number comment(modelName) <- "./AKAR4cl.so" # this is sufficient if the .so file already exists

title: Build and simulate your own Model
text: following commands allow simulate multiple parameters (saved matrix P) time. Let’s plot trajectories one picture:  new result (just like old one) array three indexes: output function j time index k selects trajectories belong P[,k] Clean-(delete temporary files):
code: t <- as.numeric(ex[[1]]$outputTimes) np <- NROW(par) REPS <- 50 P <- matrix(runif(np*REPS,min=0,max=as.numeric(par)*2),np,REPS) dim(P) #> [1]  3 50   stm <- Sys.time() sr <- sim(P) etm <- Sys.time() difftime(etm,stm) #> Time difference of 2.111909 secs T <- rep(c(t,NA),REPS)                                  # the NA value will break the line Z <- as.numeric(sr[[1]]$func[1,c(seq_along(t),NA),])    # at the end, so it doesn't loop plot(T,Z,type='l') sr[[l]]$func[i,j,k] # a number gf <- sprintf("AKAR4cl%s",c(".vf",".tar.gz",".zip",".so")) print(gf) #> [1] "AKAR4cl.vf"     "AKAR4cl.tar.gz" "AKAR4cl.zip"    "AKAR4cl.so" file.remove(gf) #> [1] TRUE TRUE TRUE TRUE

what: Uncertainty Quantification and Global Sensitivity Analysis
title: Uncertainty Quantification and Global Sensitivity Analysis
text: R package performs parameter estimation, uncertainty quantification, global sensitivity analysis using Bayesian (UQ) variance decomposition methods (GSA). primarily designed biochemical reaction networks describing intracellular cellular pathways similar. information model well experimental data used parameter estimation uncertainty quantification stored SBtab table format Systems Biology projects. Source code: https://github.com/icpm-kth/uqsa/ Documentation https://icpm-kth.github.io/uqsa/ Examples: https://icpm-kth.github.io/uqsa/articles/examples_overview.html Cite: https://icpm-kth.github.io/uqsa/articles/cite.html

title: Uncertainty Quantification and Global Sensitivity Analysis
text: open source software code developed support Swedish e-Science Research Centre (SeRC) well within Human Brain Project, funded European Union’s Horizon 2020 Framework Programme Research Innovation (945539) (Human Brain Project SGA1, SGA2 SGA3).

what: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation Sampling of Model Parameters — ABCMCMC
title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation Sampling of Model Parameters — ABCMCMC
text: Given set simulation experiments (list), model, parameter boundaries, function draw sample parameters posterior probability density given problem.

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation Sampling of Model Parameters — ABCMCMC
code: ABCMCMC(   objectiveFunction,   startPar,   nSims,   Sigma0,   delta,   dprior,   batchSize = 100,   parAcceptable = function(p) {      all(is.finite(p))  },   allow.reg = FALSE )

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation Sampling of Model Parameters — ABCMCMC
text: objectiveFunction function , given (vectorial) parameter input, simulates model, outputs distance experimental data data simulated model parameter provided input startPar starting value parameter vector nSims requested sample size Sigma0 multivariate normal covariance Markov chain transition kernel delta ABC acceptance threshold dprior function returns prior probability density values batchSize number points produce record one point parAcceptable function can reject parameter vector early based user-requirements. return scalar boolean. allow.reg allow regularization (logical), TRUE, Sigma made smaller low acceptance rate detected: one accepted update per batch

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation Sampling of Model Parameters — ABCMCMC
text: list containing sample matrix vector scores (values delta sample)

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation Sampling of Model Parameters — ABCMCMC
text: Normally, ABC produce highly auto-correlated sample, wasting lots disk-space; batchSize can used thin sample, recording every batchSize-th point: batchSize=100, perform 100 updates Markov chain variable save state sample. Higher batchSize numbers improve apparent quality sample, create work per sampled point.

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation Sampling of Model Parameters — ABCMCMC
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   f <- uqsa_example("AKAR4")   m <- model_from_tsv(f)   o <- as_ode(m)   ex <- experiments(m,o)   C <- generate_code(o)   c_path(o) <- write_c_code(C)   so_path(o) <- shlib(o)   s <- simulator.c(ex,o)   objFunc <- makeObjective(ex,s)   startPar <- values(m$Parameter)   lowerBound <- startPar - m$Paramster$stdv # m$Parameter$min   upperBound <- startPar + m$Paramster$stdv # m$Parameter$max   abcSample <- ABCMCMC(     objFunc,     startPar=values(m$Parameter),     100,     cov(rprior(1000)),     delta=1,     dprior=dUniformPrior(lowerBound,upperBound),     batchSize = 100   ) } # }

what: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation as a Particle Filter — ABCSMC
title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation as a Particle Filter — ABCSMC
text: Given set simulation experiments (list), model, parameter boundaries, function draw sample parameters posterior probability density given problem.

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation as a Particle Filter — ABCSMC
code: ABCSMC(   objectiveFunction,   startPar,   Sigma = 2 * cov(startPar),   dprior,   delta = c(2, 0.5),   parAcceptable = function(p) {      all(is.finite(p))  },   messages = FALSE )

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation as a Particle Filter — ABCSMC
text: objectiveFunction function can simulate model batch parameter vectors provided matrix columns (batches) startPar matrix shape desired sample, transposed, can sample prior pre-conditioned sample approximates posterior, e.g.: t(rprior(1000)) Sigma multivariate normal covariance Markov chain transition kernel dprior function returns prior probability density values delta ABC acceptance threshold, either scalar, initial value delta, pair values, starting value final value delta: c(initialDelta,finalDelta) parAcceptable rejection-shortcut function; parAcceptable(p) returns FALSE specific value p, means simulations even attempted. messages logical value indicating whether log messages printed

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation as a Particle Filter — ABCSMC
text: list containing sample matrix vector scores (values delta sample)

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation as a Particle Filter — ABCSMC
text: variant ABC entire batch simulated one call simulator. startPar initial batch simulated: matrix columns different parameter vectors (e.g. prior sample members). words: startPar[,] must valid argument objectiveFunction. objective function closure Objective-Function objectiveFuntion(P) return matrix n rows, n number simulation experiments (thus data-sets), m columns, m number parameterizations NCOL(P).

title: Performs and Approximate Bayesian Computation as a Particle Filter — ABCSMC
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   library(parallel)   f <- uqsa_example("AKAR4")   m <- model_from_tsv(f)   ex <- experiments(m,as_ode(m,cla=FALSE))   G <- as_cme(m)         # for Gillespie solver   C <- generate_code(G)   c_path(G) <- write_c_code(C)   so_path(G) <- shlib(G)   muX <- m$Parameter$value   sdX <- m$Parameter$stdv   rprior <- rNormalPrior(log(muX^2/(muX^2+sdX^2)),sqrt(log(1+sdX^2/muX^2)))   dprior <- dNormalPrior(log(muX^2/(muX^2+sdX^2)),sqrt(log(1+sdX^2/muX^2)))   s <- simstoch(ex,G,logParMap)   O <- makeObjective(ex,s)   X <- rprior(1000)   colnames(X) <- rownames(m$Parameter)   posterior <- ABCSMC(O,t(X),Sigma=cov(X),dprior=dprior,delta=c(0.5,1.5)) } # }

title: Collect statistical Replicas — gatherReplicas
text: function uses mclapply process files, may quicker gatherSample.  temperature beta disregarded, assuming parallel tempering used.  facilitate loading big sample, function analyse auto-correltation within file returned thinned sub-sample size N/(2*tau_int) (returning effective sample size). value tau_int calculated likelihood values, either hadron package, bultin acf function. need reduce result. small samples, better load entire sample analyse full. function intended samples big challenge memory machine. function quicker used trivial parallelism, without MPI communication ranks (another method obtaining several replicas, like forking sequential repetition). function assumes supplied RDS file contains matrix model MCMC parameters. returned value X similar effect Reduce(...,rbind) smaller samples contained individual files. value X several attributes attached : logLikelihood: log(likelihood(X[,])), one value per row X stepSize: MCMC step size used given file#'

title: Collect statistical Replicas — gatherReplicas
code: rprior <- rNormalPrior(seq(3),seq(4,5)) # some nonsense N <- 100 f <- c(tempfile(),tempfile())  ## first fake sample X <- rprior(N) attr(X,"acceptanceRate") <- 0.23 ## fake auto-correlation attr(X,"logLikelihood") <- sqrt(seq(N)) + rnorm(N,-100,3) saveRDS(X,file=f[1])  ## second fake sample X <- rprior(N) attr(X,"acceptanceRate") <- 0.23 attr(X,"logLikelihood") <- sqrt(seq(N)) + rnorm(N,-100,3) saveRDS(X,file=f[2])  Z <- gatherReplicas(f) print(N) #> [1] 100 print(dim(Z)) #> [1] 49  3 print(names(attributes(Z))) #> [1] "dim"           "logLikelihood" "stepSize"      "tau"

title: Default gradient-log-likelihood Function — gllf
text: function take log-likelihood gradient values claculated ode solver package, return sum vectors experiments. gll-value simulator returns calculated assumption normal distribution measurement errors, uses "identity" map MCMC parameters model-parameters default (.e. transformation). Like ll function almost work, merely sums gradient values calculated simulation, also performs transformation gradient vector, taking parameter-mapping sampling-space model-parameter-space account. returned function takes one argument, MCMC variable parMCMC (numeric vector). variable requires smmala specific attributes.

title: Default gradient-log-likelihood Function — gllf
code: m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAR4")) o <- as_ode(m) c_path(o) <- write_c_code(generate_code(o)) so_path(o) <- shlib(o) ex <- experiments(m,o) s <- simulator.c(ex,o,omit=1) # not 3 p <- values(m$Parameter) attr(p,"simulations") <- s(p) print(ll(p)) #> [1] -2491.245 trivialJac <- \(x) diag(1,length(x),length(x)) # the default gll <- gllf(parMapJac=trivialJac) print(gll(p)) #> [1] 1643.53085001   -2.87257643    0.03029852

title: Global Sensitivity Analysis — gsa_binning
code: rprior <- rNormalPrior(c(-1,0,1),c(1,2,3))   X <- rprior(10000)   colnames(X) <- LETTERS[seq(3)]   Z <- exp(X[,1,drop=FALSE]+X[,2,drop=FALSE])   colnames(Z) <- "alpha"   GSA <- gsa_binning(X,Z)   print(GSA) #>               A         B            C #> alpha 0.2412941 0.1565027 0.0003389213   cat("global sensitivity of alpha with respect to B: ",GSA['alpha','B'],"\n") #> global sensitivity of alpha with respect to B:  0.1565027

text: list sensitivity indices $SI total sensitivities $SIT

title: Outputs the global sensitivity scores SI and SIT, calculated by the Sobol-Homma-Saltelli method — gsa_saltelli
text: matrices shaped similarly M1, M2 N respectively, now parameters replaced effects observable interest (output). can vector valued output specific (single) time-point scalar output different time-points. See Geir Halnes et al. (Halnes, Geir, et al. J. comp. neuroscience 27.3 (2009): 471.

title: Outputs the global sensitivity scores SI and SIT, calculated by the Sobol-Homma-Saltelli method — gsa_saltelli
code: m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAR4")) o <- write_and_compile(as_ode(m)) ex <- experiments(m,o) s <- simulator.c(ex[1],o) p0 <- values(m$Parameter) rprior <- rUniformPrior(p0/2,p0*2) SP <- saltelli_prior(1000,rprior) fM1 <- t(s(t(SP$M1))[[1]]$func[1,,]) fM2 <- t(s(t(SP$M2))[[1]]$func[1,,]) fN <- lapply(asplit(SP$N,3),\(N) t(s(t(N))[[1]]$func[1,,])) fN <- simplify2array(fN) GSA <- gsa_saltelli(fM1,fM2,fN) print(names(GSA)) #> [1] "SI"  "SIT" cat(   "average relative senitivity S(p1) / S(p2): ",   mean(abs(GSA$SI[,1]/GSA$SI[,2]),na.rm=TRUE) ) #> average relative senitivity S(p1) / S(p2):  14864.47

code: gsl_odeiv2_CRNN(   name,   experiments,   l,   nu,   m,   abs.tol = 1e-06,   rel.tol = 1e-05,   initial.step.size = 0.001,   method = 0,   time.out = 1,   nstep = 0 )

title: simulates a CRNN ode model with extra work — gsl_odeiv2_CRNN
text: name either name file (shared library file) name ODE model simulate (shared library name dynamically loaded needs created first). name model given, file must name current directory comment indicates location. experiments list N simulation experiments (time, parameters, initial value, events). l matrix parameters M columns, log-space. nu stoichiometry matrix (N × R) N number state variables R number reactions, reactions assumed reversible. m modifiers – similar stoichiometry, indicates whether species takes part reaction without consumed. abs.tol absolute tolerance, real scalar. rel.tol relative tolerance, real scalar. initial.step.size initial value step size; step size adapt value observes tolerances, real scalar. method one integration methods bundled GSL (see method name_method). time.time limit seconds, checked every measurement time-point (data). nstep maximum number ODE integrator steps, checked every step, defaults unlimited (0).

title: simulates a CRNN ode model with extra work — gsl_odeiv2_CRNN
text: list solution trajectories y(t;p) experiments (named like experiments), well output functions.

title: simulates a CRNN ode model with extra work — gsl_odeiv2_CRNN
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{   f <- uqsa_example("AKAR4")   m <- model_from_tsv(f)   ex <- experiments(m,as_ode(m,cla=FALSE))   nu <- stoichiometric_matrix(m)   l <- matrix(c(log(values(m$Parameter)),0),2,2,dimnames=list(rownames(m$Reaction),c("fwd","bwd")))   C <- CRNN(NCOL(nu),initialValues=values(m$Compound),funcValues=formulae(m$Output))   c.file <- tempfile("AKAR4_",fileext=".c")   cat(C,file=c.file,sep='\n')   so.file <- shlib(c.file,model.name="AKAR4")   y <- gsl_odeiv2_CRNN(so.file,ex,l,nu,nu*0) } # }

text: odeModel name ODE model simulate (shared library name dynamically loaded needs created first). Alternatively can ode object created as_ode, shared library path attached . experiments list N simulation experiments (time, parameters, initial value, events) p matrix parameters M columns abs.tol absolute tolerance, real scalar rel.tol relative tolerance, real scalar initial.step.size initial value step size; step size adapt value observes tolerances, real scalar method integration method (see method name_method). omit integer indicates many omit order: fisher information, gradient log-likelihood, log-likelihood time.seconds (early rejection due long simulation time). can trigger measurement times (outputTime). num.steps maximum number steps integration method permitted ; early rejection. condition can trigger point integration.

title: simulates an ode model with extra work — gsl_odeiv2_fi
text: model always simulated using shared library. path shared library can passed three different ways: Character vector: odeModel <- c("AKAKR4","/tmp/path/AKAR4.") comment: comment(odeModel) <- "/tmp/path/AKAR4." part ode object:   shared library needs created first. Either R CMD SHLIB, shlib, manually system's command line (bash, zsh, etc.).
code: odeModel <- as_ode(m)` so_path(odeModel) <- "/tmp/path/AKAR4.so"

title: simulates an ode model with extra work — gsl_odeiv2_fi
code: # \donttest{   requireNamespace("errors")   f <- uqsa_example("AKAR4")   m <- model_from_tsv(f)   o <- as_ode(m)   ex <- experiments(m,o)   C <- generate_code(o)   c_path(o) <- write_c_code(C)   so_path(o) <- shlib(o)   print(o) #>                 Model name : AKAR4 #>                     C file : /tmp/RtmpiY0iWI/RtmpQMQrCy/adf9204aaf2748b8/AKAR4.c [2026-06-26 13:34:58.400742] #>             shared library : /tmp/RtmpiY0iWI/RtmpQMQrCy/adf9204aaf2748b8/AKAR4.so [2026-06-26 13:34:58.400742] #>  Number of state variables : 2 #>       Number of parameters : 5 #>          Number of outputs : 1 #>          Conservation laws : 2 #>            Transformations : no   y <- gsl_odeiv2_fi(o,ex,values(m$Parameter))   print(length(y)) #> [1] 3   print(names(y[[1]])) #> [1] "cpuSeconds"        "numSteps"          "status"            #> [4] "state"             "func"              "logLikelihood"     #> [7] "gradLogLikelihood" "FisherInformation"   par(mfrow=c(length(ex),1))   for (i in seq_along(y)){       plot(           errors::as.errors(ex[[i]]$outputTimes),           ex[[i]]$data,           xlab="time",           ylab=rownames(y[[i]]$data)[1],           main=names(ex)[i],           ylim=c(100,200)       )       lines(ex[[i]]$outputTimes,drop(y[[i]]$func),col='red')   }  # }

These two concepts clash with one another if a compound is conserved, but also changed by scheduled events. p <- rwm %@% "init"             # a valid starting point N <- 200 smallSample <- rwm(rwm %@% "init",N,1e-4) plot(   smallSample %@% "logLikelihood",   type='l',   main=sprintf("%i iterations",N),   xlab="iterations",   ylab="log-likelihood" )  # }

code: rprior <- rNormalPrior(seq(3),seq(4,5)) # some nonsense N <- 100 f <- c(tempfile(),tempfile())  ## first fake sample X <- rprior(N) attr(X,"beta") <- sample(1/seq(2)^2,N,replace=TRUE) attr(X,"acceptanceRate") <- 0.23 attr(X,"swapRate") <- 0.1 attr(X,"logLikelihood") <- rnorm(N,-100,30) saveRDS(X,file=f[1])  ## second fake sample X <- rprior(N) attr(X,"beta") <- sample(1/seq(2)^2,N,replace=TRUE) attr(X,"acceptanceRate") <- 0.23 attr(X,"swapRate") <- 0.1 attr(X,"logLikelihood") <- rnorm(N,-100,30) saveRDS(X,file=f[2])  Z <- loadSample_mpi(f) #> loading sample files with acceptances: #> [1] 0.23 0.23 print(dim(Z$Sample)) #> [1] 200   3 print(names(Z)) #> [1] "Sample"         "beta"           "acceptanceRate" "swapRate"       #> [5] "logLikelihood"  "betaSelection"  "uB"

Supply function experiments need normalized experiments (complex cases). simpleUserLLF (optional) user supplied function used instead default sum ((y-h)/stdv)^2 terms. interface : simpleUserLLF(y,h,stdv,name=NULL), N-M-matrix N dimensionality model output M number data time-points.  , y t(experiments[[]]$data) may contain NA values.  function also accept optional name argument (name experiment function currently called ).

title: Default log-likelihood function — logLikelihoodFunc
text: llf(parMCMC), closure (function) mcmc-variable: parMCMC; returns scalar log-likelihood value. Alternatively, user can define function: parMCMC -> log(Likelihood(parMCMC)), use sampling. test simulation p: y <- simulate(p) reveal values simulator produces. values attached p sampling, attribute.  mcmc_init attach values initial Markov chain state.  log-likelihood function can use attributes.

title: Default log-likelihood function — logLikelihoodFunc
code: m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAR4")) o <- as_ode(m) c_path(o) <- write_c_code(generate_code(o)) so_path(o) <- shlib(o) ex <- experiments(m,o) s <- simulator.c(ex,o,omit=0) p <- values(m$Parameter) attr(p,"simulations") <- s(p) ## this function is fairly flexible and accepts some user settings llf <- logLikelihoodFunc(ex) #> experiments contain 675 non-missing values print(llf(p)) #> Warning: In 'Ops' : non-'errors' operand automatically coerced to an 'errors' object with no uncertainty #> [1] -2491.245 ## this function uses the values from the solver: print(ll(p)) #> [1] -2491.245

title: Initialize the Markov chain — mcmc_init
code: m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAR4")) o <- as_ode(m) p0 <- values(m$Parameter) c_path(o) <- write_c_code(generate_code(o)) so_path(o) <- shlib(o) ex <- experiments(m,o) s <- simfi(ex,o) dprior <- dNormalPrior(p0,m$Parameter$stdv) p <- mcmc_init(1.0,p0,s,dprior=dprior) print(names(attributes(p))) ## now has attributes necessary for MCMC #> [1] "names"         "unit"          "beta"          "simulations"   #> [5] "prior"         "logLikelihood" "class"

title: Metropolis Update is an MCMC update function — metropolis_update
text: simulate function simulates model logLikelihood function returns log-likelihood value given paramegter value, simulations attached parameter attreibute (probably closure) dprior function returns prior density given parameter vector Sigma transition kernel's covariance matrix. parAcceptable function can used reject proposal based values parameters alone (shortcut rejection, sans simulation)

title: Metropolis Update is an MCMC update function — metropolis_update
text: Using simulations, acceptance rule, proposed update either accepted rejected. function returns closure metropolis, parMCMC sole argument: parProposal <- metropolis(parGiven) optional argument function parAcceptable, sampling, metropolis called update function, parAcceptable(parProposal) returns FALSE, metropolis shortcuts retrun(parGiven) without performing simulations. function can used weed parameter combinations result obviously nonsensical simulations without wasting CPU-time.

title: Metropolis Update is an MCMC update function — metropolis_update
code: m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAR4")) o <- as_ode(m) c_path(o) <- write_c_code(generate_code(o)) so_path(o) <- shlib(o) ex <- experiments(m,o) s <- simulator.c(ex,o,omit=0) dprior <- dNormalPrior(values(m$Parameter),m$Parameter$stdv) p <- mcmc_init(1.0,values(m$Parameter),s,ll,dprior) UP <- metropolis_update(s,ll,dprior=dprior,Sigma=diag(m$Parameter$stdv)^2) p2 <- UP(p) ## updated value: print(p2) #> kf_C_AKAR4 kb_C_AKAR4 kcat_AKARp  #>      0.018      0.106     10.200  #>              simulations: 3 (length) #>            logLikelihood: -2491.25 #>                    prior: 10.5823 print(sum(abs(p2-p))) #> [1] 0

title: Determine a starting value for ABC's delta — preCalibration
text: objectiveFunction function , given (vectorial) parameter input, (1) simulates model given parameter, (2) outputs distance experimental data simulated data simulated. npc sample size pre-calibration. rprior function generates random ABC variables, distributed according prior. rep number repetitions preCalibration process. p fraction (top scoring) sampled points base Sigma (Sigma covariance matrix moves proposed ABCMCMC algorithm). sfactor scales Sigma (Sigma covariance matrix moves proposed ABCMCMC algorithm). delta ABC threshold. num number different starting parameter vectors (initial states chains) generate. Usually, num equal number chain run sampling procedure.

title: Determine a starting value for ABC's delta — preCalibration
text: list entries prePar (sampled parameters),  preDelta (distances experimental data trajectories produced parameters prePar), Sigma (covariance matrix moves proposed ABCMCMC algortihm) startPar (starting parameters ABCMCMC chains)

title: Determine a starting value for ABC's delta — preCalibration
code: m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAR4")) cme <- as_cme(m) ex <- experiments(m) C <- generate_code(cme) c_path(cme) <- write_c_code(C) so_path(cme) <- shlib(cme) p0 <- log10(values(m$Parameter)) s <- simstoch(ex,cme,parMap=log10ParMap) Obj <- makeObjective(ex,s) rprior <- rNormalPrior(p0,0.5) PC <- preCalibration(Obj,50,rprior,delta=1) # should be more than 50 #> Warning: distances between experiment and simulation are too big; selecting the best (50) parameter vectors. print(names(PC)) #> [1] "prePar"   "preDelta" "Sigma"    "startPar" print(PC$Sigma) #>             [,1]        [,2]        [,3] #> [1,] 0.004496082 0.001051894 0.003292040 #> [2,] 0.001051894 0.024296263 0.005356373 #> [3,] 0.003292040 0.005356373 0.029494625

title: scrnn returns a closure around gsl_odeiv2_CRNN() — scrnn
code: # \donttest{   f <- uqsa_example("AKAR4")   m <- model_from_tsv(f)   ex <- experiments(m)   nu <- stoichiometric_matrix(m)   l <- matrix(     c(log(values(m$Parameter)),-1e6),     2,2,     byrow=TRUE,     dimnames=list(rownames(m$Reaction),c("fwd","bwd"))   )   C <- CRNN(     NCOL(nu),     initialValues=values(m$Compound),     funcValues=formulae(m$Output)   )   c.file <- tempfile("AKAR4",fileext=".c")   cat(C,file=c.file,sep='\n')   modelName <- "CRNN"   comment(modelName) <- shlib(c.file)   s <- scrnn(ex, modelName)   p <- list(l=l,nu=nu,m=nu*0)   y <- s(p)   plot(ex,y)    #> NULL # }

text: context, sensitivity S(t;x,p) dx(t;p)/dp, x(t;p) parameterized solution initial value problem ordinary differential equations t independent varibale: x'=f(t,x;p), «'» indicates derivative respect t. cases proxy variable p, e.g. r=log(p), chain rule applies. Similarly, also output sensitivity function g(x(t;p)).  equilibrium approximation exact state-variable values close equilibrium point q(p) (fixed-point): f(t,q(p);p)=0.

title: Equilibrium state approximation of the solution sensitivity for ODE systems — sensitivityEquilibriumApproximation
text: state sensitivity matrix:   param raw model parameters. matrix calculated intermediate transformed :   parMCMC Markov chain variable usually shorter param typically sample model's parameters. model parameters may known, may input parameters intrinsic model related experimental setup (parMCMC param different). transformation requires output function jacobian (funcJac) parameter jacobian (funcJacp) model variable. transform parameters , chain rule requests parMapJacl,k = d paraml / d parMCMCk Typically, sensitivity needs known different time-points t_k. 3-dimensional array Si,j,k, index k corrsponds time t_k; closer x(t_k) equilibrium, better approximation; near initial state, sensitivity also correct (intermediate time-span approximate). function requires pracma::expm work. f

title: This creates a closure that simulates the model, similar to simulator.c — simfi
text: experiments list experiments simulate: inital values, inputs, time vectors, initial times odeModel Either ode object created as_ode (shared library field inserted), string (comment indicating .file) points model simulate parMap model called parMap(parABC); parameter transformation can happen . method integration method integer (higher numbers simpler methods, lower numbers advanced methods, 0 maps 'msbdf') omit integer, omit optional return values, order: Fisher Information, gradient log-likelihood, log-likelihood, output functions. Omission includes previous entries. omit = 1 omits Fisher Information, omit=3, omits FI, grad-ll, log-likelihood calculations. time.(seconds); simulations aborted time greater . num.steps unlimited default, setting finite value can help stop stiff simulations early.

title: This creates a closure that simulates the model, similar to simulator.c — simfi
text: returns closure around: - experiments, - model, - parameter mapping returned function depends parABC (sampling parameters). version function use parallel package add noise simulations (unlike simulator.c). hopeless simulation can stopped early using settings num.steps time.. value num.steps applies every continuous simulation stretch (e.g. betwee two events), teh count steps reset whenever event occurs one simulation ends (different parameters different experiments). time.given seconds can trigger measurement time points (t_wallclock > time.), points. much time passed checked integrator stopped record state. limit number steps, hand, feature GSL ODE solvers can trigger precisely.

title: SMMALA Update is an MCMC update function — smmala_update
code: smmala_update(   simulate,   logLikelihood = ll,   dprior = function(x) prod(dnorm(x)),   gradLogLikelihood = gllf(log10ParMapJac),   gprior = function(x) (-x),   fisherInformation = fi(log10ParMapJac),   fisherInformationPrior = 0,   parAcceptable = function(p) all(is.finite(p)) )

title: SMMALA Update is an MCMC update function — smmala_update
text: simulate function simulates model logLikelihood function returns log-likelihood value given paramegter value, simulations attached parameter attreibute (probably closure) dprior function returns prior density given parameter vector gradLogLikelihood function calculates estimates gradient log-likelihood function, chosen parameter mapping. Function must take one argument (MCMC variable) gprior function returns gradient log-prior distribution. fisherInformation function estimates Fisher Information given MCMC variable (parMCMC). fisherInformationPrior constant fisherInformation prior distribution (rather, precision prior) parAcceptable function can used reject proposal based values parameters alone (shortcut rejection, sans simulation)

title: SMMALA Update is an MCMC update function — smmala_update
text: Using simulations, acceptance rule, proposed update either accepted rejected. function returns closure smmala, parMCMC sole argument: parProposal <- smmala(parGiven) optional argument function parAcceptable, sampling, metropolis called update function, parAcceptable(parProposal) returns FALSE, metropolis shortcuts retrun(parGiven) without performing simulations. function can used weed parameter combinations result obviously nonsensical simulations without wasting CPU-time. argument fisherInformationPrior really precision prior (constant matrix). role additive fisherInformation used regularize final Fisher Information Matrix (makes invertible).

title: SMMALA Update is an MCMC update function — smmala_update
code: m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAR4")) o <- as_ode(m) c_path(o) <- write_c_code(generate_code(o)) so_path(o) <- shlib(o) ex <- experiments(m,o) s <- simulator.c(ex,o,omit=0) ### without parameter transformations gll <- gllf() FI <- fi() dprior <- dNormalPrior(values(m$Parameter),m$Parameter$stdv) gprior <- dNormalPrior(values(m$Parameter),m$Parameter$stdv) p <- mcmc_init(1.0,values(m$Parameter),s,ll,dprior,gll,gprior,FI) UP <- smmala_update(s,ll,dprior=dprior,gll,gprior=gprior,FI,solve(diag(m$Parameter$stdv))) p2 <- UP(p) ## updated value: print(p2) #> [1]  0.01801046  0.10893704 10.21378666 #>              simulations: 3 (length) #>            logLikelihood: -2491.24 #>                    prior: 10.5776 #>        gradLogLikelihood: 3 (length) #>             gradLogPrior: 10.5776 #>        fisherInformation: 9 (length) print(sum(abs(p2-p))) #> [1] 0.01673416

code: # \donttest{ m <- model_from_tsv(uqsa_example("AKAP79")) rwm <- high_level_metropolis(m) # "random walk", metropolis algorithm #> The parameters are given in log10-scale, so the simulator will do the reverse transformation: 10^p. p <- rwm %@% "init"             # a valid starting point h <- tune_step_size(rwm,p) #> acceptance rate: 0.14, step-size: 0.0001; #> acceptance rate: 0.2, step-size: 4.77467e-05; #> acceptance rate: 0.25, step-size: 3.72657e-05; N <- 200 smallSample <- rwm(rwm %@% "init",N,h) print(h) #> [1] 3.726568e-05 plot(   smallSample %@% "logLikelihood",   type="l",   main=sprintf("step size: %g",h),   xlab="iterations",   ylab="log-likelihood" )  # }

what: uqsa: Uncertainty Quantification and Global Sensitivity Analysis — uqsa-package
title: uqsa: Uncertainty Quantification and Global Sensitivity Analysis — uqsa-package
text: field systems biology, chemical reaction networks modeled various ways, two : () stochastic simulations (e.g. Gillespie algorithm) (ii) ordinary differential equations. package use simple tabular model description reaction systems automatically generate C code either solver type. use ODE solvers GNU Scientific Library provide interface deals lists simulation experiments. simulation experiment contains data, instructions model replicate data. use ABC methods (combined MCMC SMC) well classic MCMC methods Random Walk Metropolis (Gaussian transition kernel) SMMALA (Simplified Manifold Metropolis adjusted Langevin algorithm) Bayesian investigation model´s parameter space. Experiments can evaluated sequence; intermediate probability densities modeled using VineCopula package. UQSA also intended useful HPC environment, functions use pbdMPI capabilities.
